data.title: Restaurant Brands International: A Shakier Bet As Rivals Pull Ahead (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
If there’s one thing most investors can likely agree on, it’s that the stock market in 2026 has proven to be exceptionally difficult to invest in. Amid market volatility, this is not a time for passive index investing: in my view, we should be deploying careful stock picking to buy deep bargains (many in the tech sector, after the AI-driven rout) while also culling out names from our portfolios where recent fundamentals are beginning to wane.

Restaurant Brands International (QSR), the parent company behind Burger King and Tim Hortons, is one of those companies that looks like it's on shakier footing. After stronger comp sales earlier in the year, RBI closed out Q4 with a whimper, while fast food giant McDonald's (MCD) is pulling ahead. Amid ever-increasing indication that the fast food industry is becoming more price-competitive, it's time to take caution on this stock after its recent 5%+ post-earnings pullback.

I last wrote a buy article on Restaurant Brands International in early January, when the stock was trading just shy of $70. Since then, RBI's Q4 earnings print, which showcased a slowdown in sales across all of its major brands, has made me more jittery about the impacts of the macroeconomy on dine-in traffic and the increased aggression of rivals to grab price-conscious consumers. With this in mind, I'm dropping my rating on RBI to neutral.

At current share prices, I see more of a balanced bull and bear case for RBI. On the bright side for the company:

  • International expansion surge. While comp sales in the U.S. are slowing down thanks to a more unfavorable macroeconomy, Restaurant Brands is achieving far more solid, high single-digit comps growth in international markets. It's also broadening its footprint for its concepts overseas, with the company growing the number of Tim Hortons and Popeyes locations overseas by 9% y/y and 26% y/y, respectively.
  • Margin expansion. Even in spite of comps sluggishness in the U.S./Canada markets, RBI's increasing mix of franchise sales is helping the company to drive operating margin expansion, defying ingredient inflation and labor rate increases.

At the same time, however, we have to be wary of the following red flags:

  • Competitors are getting much more aggressive on price competition. The restaurant sector has seen a hollowing out of the mid-tier “fast casual” names like Chipotle (CMG) and CAVA (CAVA), as consumers respond to price increases by visiting less frequently (as evidenced by declining comp sales and traffic metrics). Conversely, McDonald's has grabbed share by emphasizing promotions, especially its nationally advertised $5 Meal Deal. RBI is facing a much more promotional sales environment.
  • Huge debt load. RBI is shouldering a net debt load of over $12.5 billion, which represents greater than a 4x leverage ratio against trailing adjusted EBITDA. Most commercial banks typically consider 3x to be the upper threshold at which a company is highly leveraged.

Against the counterbalancing rise of RBI's risk factors, I find the stock's valuation fair, but not entirely compelling. As shown below, the stock trades at a 16x P/E ratio and a 12x forward adjusted EBITDA ratio, on the lower end relative to peers (only more expensive than Wendy's (WEN), which is struggling with top-line declines). That said, at this juncture I'd rather pay a slight premium for a more surefire bet like McDonald's than make the bet that RBI can maintain market share in its critical home markets.

My recommendation here is to reduce allocation to RBI and wait until either we see more of a green light from comps trends or until RBI's share price drops much further.

Q4 recap

Let's now go through RBI's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

RBI's Q4 revenue grew 7.4% y/y to $2.47 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $2.41 billion (+5.1% y/y) by a respectable two-point margin. The growth was fairly split between ~3% comparable sales growth as well as ~3% net restaurant growth, mostly concentrated in international markets (especially from the company's smaller concepts, which are Popeyes and Firehouse Subs), which led to 5.8% year-over-year growth in systemwide sales globally across all brands, to $12.13 billion.

Where we are paying the most attention, however, is to the company's comp sales trends (often called same-restaurant sales by other companies, and which, as a reminder, measures revenue growth from existing restaurant locations and excludes the contribution of newly opened or recently closed stores).

The chart below showcases comp trends for the company's domestic businesses. Tim Hortons, which is the company's largest brand by revenue (due to a higher proportion of company-owned stores), saw home market (Canada) growth rates cut in half sequentially, to 2.4% y/y in Q4, versus 4.8% in Q3. Burger King, which is the company's largest brand by overall system sales (and its second largest in revenue), saw comps slow to 2.6%, 60bps weaker relative to 3.2% growth in Q3. Popeyes and Firehouse Subs, which together account for just over 10% of overall company revenue in FY25, also saw minor slowdowns.

We do note that growth rates are still stronger than the first half of the year. Also, RBI has made up for domestic weakness by showcasing stronger growth in international markets. As shown below, Burger King saw stronger 5.8% y/y growth overseas (more than double its U.S. performance), while Popeyes accelerated to 12% y/y growth.

Where we are concerned, however, is the comparison versus the industry leader: McDonald's. In Q4, McDonald's delivered startling 5.7% y/y overall comps growth and 6.8% y/y growth in the U.S., which accelerated 210bps and 440bps versus 3.6% y/y overall and 2.4% y/y U.S. comps in Q3, respectively.

Now, we caution against reading too much into a single quarter's trends. But we also can't ignore the fact that that performance is diverging between Burger King and McDonald's. The latter is accelerating sharply, and the snapshot above specifically calls out both positive check increases (price/mix benefits) and traffic counts driven by successful promotions. We're impressed that promotional activity has not impacted the average check. All indications point to the fact that, at least domestically, McDonald's is taking market share.

We note that RBI still drove meaningful margin expansion. Adjusted operating income rose ~16% y/y to $674 million, reflecting a 27.3% margin that improved 2 points versus 25.1% in the year-ago quarter.

As shown in the chart below, in FY25 RBI delivered $1.45 billion of free cash flow, up 11% y/y.

And while the company has made progress in a slight deleveraging over the past year, we still note its year-end 4.2x leverage ratio skews high.

Key takeaways

Q4 has clearly demonstrated something we've always known: fast food is an incredibly competitive industry. Lately, we've seen sharper impacts of McDonald's leaning into pricing and promotions to drive traffic and market share. While Restaurant Brands remains attractively priced, its appeal is waning as domestic comps slow. For now, maintain caution and move to the sidelines.
